item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: She'd probably last about a minute or so, enough for able to be mildly impressed. Then she'd get stomped. Of course if this was back when people actually knew her name, that might be a fair fight.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I know I shouldn't… But holy crap, I wanna watch her fight Able.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's Able, except guns really, really work. Love it. +1

Definite improvement from the first draft. I think the tweaks you made to the agents to make them more professional-acting, at least to a degree, makes them a much better straight-man to the absurdity of the deity.

I still feel they're a bit too light-hearted given the capabilities of this anomaly - I mean, sure, they've encountered "Gods" before, but they're still very non-nonchalant about a known mass-murderer. That, and the test logs still feel unnecessary. You're not explaining any new mechanics about the anomaly integral to the plot, so they're just more padding. It's not bad, per say, since they're fun to read, but they could still be more.

Still, the improvements you've made from draft really pushed this over the top. Good work!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not the author, but to be fair I think her dialogue was meant to be that way. It appeals to a certain sense of humor that you see in things like Undertale or Over The Garden Wall, which is what gives the article such a charming feel. If her dialogue was grandiose and "goddess"-like, the article itself wouldn't be nearly as good. She's charming, funny and likeable, and ties the skip as a whole together nicely.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No-vote. I like the concept, but Sandraugia's dialog seems far too conversational and mundane for a forgotten goddess. She needs to be grandiose. For instance:

Romero: Oh. Do the Netherlands even have sand?

SCP-3928-1: Well we have beaches, so yes.

Doesn't sound like a phrase spoken by a god that's mad that it's being forgotten, and that its signature weapon's role is being replaced by far less elegant weaponry. It sounds like a civilian clarifying that, yes, being a coastal country, the Netherlands has beaches and therefore sand.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I care her. +1

(hey, maybe she could join that new mtf they were making in the resurrection canon? that'd be a fun tale)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ha, that was good. Happy to see it back up.

(Addressed!)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Whilst the first version of this article felt very dry, and terse, this version was anything but.

The additional interview logs and iteration, as well as the revision that the entity dislikes all forms of firearms, even ones that don't exist really extended the piece and gave it a satisfying resolution.

+1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Excellent work, I really enjoyed reading this. Well done.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a marked improvement over the original, and the dialogue had some good moments. I'm glad you took the time to improve your piece.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I remember bringing my son to the local con and seeing him becoming fascinated by the swords for sale. This really brought that back. Thanks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I’m really proud of for sizing up what you first posted, seeing it was lacking, and coming back to turn something that was thoroughly mediocre into something highly original and far from boring. I think the section before the logs could do without the specific media references but that’s really my only nitpick.

Well done!

item.title: Sword 2: Sword Harder
item.content:encoded:
Sword.

Yup, this again.

Thank you to everyone who critted this work, whether it was this version or a previous one. Wouldn't be here without you.

Photo is from here.

While you're here, be sure to check out some of my other works.
